**THE INTERVIEW - B1 LEVEL (5 minutes)**
**Target:** 12-16 words per sentence, max 25 words
**Connectors:** More complex connectors (however, meanwhile, therefore, although, because)
**Word count target:** ~500-550 words

---

Carlos sat at his computer and stared at the screen, reading another rejection email that arrived in his inbox. This was the 46th rejection he had received since arriving in America six months ago, and each one felt like a punch to his confidence. However, he had one more interview scheduled for tomorrow morning, and this was truly his last chance to find a job before his savings ran out completely.

When Carlos first came to America, he knew his English was not perfect, but he believed that hard work and determination would be enough. He studied English every single day, practicing conversations with strangers and watching English videos late into the night. Despite all his efforts, employers kept telling him that his English was not good enough for their positions, which made him question whether he would ever succeed in this new country.

The night before the interview, Carlos could not sleep because his mind was racing with worry. He practiced his answers over and over again, trying to memorize perfect responses to common interview questions. He rehearsed how to describe his work experience, his skills, and his goals for the future. However, no matter how much he practiced, he still felt unprepared and anxious about what would happen the next day.

The next morning, Carlos woke up before sunrise and put on his best suit, which he had carefully ironed the night before. He looked at himself in the mirror and tried to give himself a pep talk, telling himself that he was capable and that this time would be different. However, as he drove to the interview, his hands shook on the steering wheel, and he could feel his heart beating rapidly in his chest.

When Carlos arrived at the tall office building downtown, he took a deep breath and walked inside, trying to appear confident even though he felt terrified inside. He found the elevator and pressed the button for the 15th floor, watching the numbers light up as the elevator climbed higher and higher. When the doors opened, he walked down a long hallway and found the interview room, where three people were already waiting for him.

The interview started well enough, with Carlos answering simple questions about his background and experience. However, when one of the interviewers asked him to tell them about himself, something unexpected happened. Carlos's mind went completely blank, and he could not remember a single word he had practiced the night before. He sat there in silence, feeling panic rise in his chest as the interviewers waited patiently for his response.

Carlos felt embarrassed and wanted to run away, but he knew that he could not give up now. He closed his eyes for a moment and took several deep breaths, trying to calm his racing heart. In that moment of silence, he thought about why he had come to America in the first place, remembering his dreams and the sacrifices his family had made to support him. He thought about all the nights he had spent studying English, and he realized that he had already come so far.

When Carlos opened his eyes again, he made a decision to stop trying to be perfect and instead speak from his heart. He started talking slowly, telling them about his journey from his home country to America, about the challenges he had faced learning a new language, and about his determination to build a better life. He did not use perfect grammar or sophisticated vocabulary, but he spoke with honesty and passion, and the words flowed naturally from his heart.

The interviewers listened carefully, and Carlos could see that they were genuinely interested in what he had to say. They asked him more questions, and this time Carlos answered them honestly, sharing both his strengths and the areas where he was still learning. He showed them his passion for his work, his willingness to learn, and his determination to succeed, and they could see that these qualities mattered more than perfect English.

At the end of the interview, Carlos shook their hands and thanked them for their time, feeling proud that he had not given up even when things seemed impossible. As he walked out of the building, he did not know if he would get the job, but he felt a sense of accomplishment because he had faced his fear and spoken from his heart. Two days later, when Carlos received an email offering him the position, he realized that sometimes being authentic and determined matters more than being perfect.
